mappings.rationale: Directly addresses Western philosophy of law, jurisprudence theories, legal positivism, natural law, and the law-morality relationship (A.1).
Legal systems broadly -- including courts, lawyers, and the practice of law -- connect to outline B.4 and D.
The rule of law as a foundational principle in legal theory and its relationship to constitutional government is central to A.1.b.
Directly addresses the study of distinctions and parallels among diverse legal systems (C).
Islamic law as a major non-Western philosophy of law and legal system is central to A.2.
Islamic philosophical traditions including ethics and political philosophy inform non-Western legal thought (A.2).
The Confucian tradition is central to Chinese philosophy of law and social ordering (A.2).
The development of Anglo-American common law exemplifies a modern legal system (B.4.a).
Egyptian civilization -- including its legal customs and governance -- is relevant to ancient Egyptian law (B.2.a).
Cuneiform civilizations of Mesopotamia, including Babylonian legal codes, are foundational to B.2.b.
Ancient Greek civilization and its legal and political institutions are relevant to B.2.d.
The Hellenistic period and its legal and administrative systems connect to B.2.e.
Roman legal institutions during the Republic are foundational to Western law (B.2.f).
Roman law during the imperial period proved critical for later Continental civil law traditions (B.2.f).
Medieval European law -- feudal legal institutions, canon law, and constitutional developments -- connects to outline B.3.
A foundational document of medieval constitutional law and the common law tradition, central to B.3.b.
Natural law theory as articulated by Aquinas is a major tradition in the philosophy of law and medieval legal thought (A.1.c, A.1.d).
The philosophical relationship between law and morality and natural law principles is central to A.1.c.
Stoic natural law philosophy is foundational to Western legal theory and Roman jurisprudence (A.1.c, A.1.d).
Hobbesian legal theory -- the social contract and the basis of legal authority -- is central to A.1.d.
Lockean natural rights theory and its influence on constitutional law and legal philosophy are relevant to A.1.d.
Islamic philosophy relevant to non-Western legal thought and jurisprudence connects to A.2.
Medieval philosophical developments -- including canon law thought and natural law reasoning -- connect to B.3.d and A.1.d.
